sessions.title: Meaning of Science and Technology
indicators: Define science
Explain the meaning of technology
Describe some impacts of science and technology
sessions.content:
Definition of Science

Science is a continuing effort to discover and increase knowledge through research. Scientists make observations, record measurable data relating to their observations, and analyse the information at hand to construct theoretical explanations of the phenomenon involved.

The Scientific Method:

  • Observation: Making careful observations of natural phenomena
  • Hypothesis: Proposing a testable explanation
  • Experimentation: Conducting experiments to test the hypothesis under controlled conditions
  • Analysis: Analysing the results and drawing conclusions
  • Publication: Publishing work so other scientists can replicate and strengthen the results

Definition of Technology

Technology is the use of scientific knowledge for practical purposes in industry or in our everyday lives. Technology is an applied field concerned with developing, constructing, and applying ideas that result in apparatuses, gadgets, tools, machines, and techniques.

Key point: Whenever we use our knowledge gained in science to achieve some specific purpose, we are in the domain of technology.

Examples of technology:

  • The discovery of the wheel
  • Computers, tablets, and smartphones
  • Microwaves and MP3 players
  • Cars, aeroplanes, and locomotives
  • The light bulb and microchips
  • Space exploration technology

Impacts of Science and Technology

Positive Impacts:

  • New technologies: cars, aircraft, computers, cell phones, GPS, bio and genetic technologies
  • Improved quality of life in many areas

Negative Impacts:

  • Environmental destruction: Deforestation, soil erosion, destruction of land due to mining, manufacturing, highways, and housing
  • Extinction: Many species have become extinct
  • Overpopulation: Increased consumption and environmental destruction
  • Chemical, genetic, and nuclear pollution: Greenhouse effect, climatic changes, waste products
  • Weapons development: Technology has been used to improve weapons – killing has become psychologically easier

Quality of life should be measured by:

  • Health
  • Clean water and clean air
  • Healthy food
  • Beautiful nature
  • Creative working environment
  • Good art and culture
  • Love, good friends, and strong family

sessions.title: Contribution of Science and Technology to Food and Nutrition
indicators: Explain some of the scientific and technological processes in making food and nutrition
Give examples of processed food
sessions.content:
Food Processing

Food processing began about 2 million years ago when a distant ancestor discovered cooking, the original form of food processing. Humans first learned how to cook food, then how to transform, preserve, and store it safely.

Modern food science and technology integrate biology, chemistry, physics, engineering, materials science, microbiology, nutrition, toxicology, biotechnology, genomics, computer science, and many other disciplines to solve difficult problems.

Contributions of Science and Technology to Food

  • Food quality can be maintained or even improved
  • Food safety can be enhanced
  • Sensitive nutrients can be preserved
  • Vitamins and minerals can be added (fortification/enrichment)
  • Toxins and anti-nutrients (substances like phytate that limit bioavailability of nutrients) can be removed
  • Foods can be designed to optimize health and reduce the risk of disease
  • Waste and product loss can be reduced
  • Distribution around the world can be facilitated

Evolution of Food Processing Methods

  • Cooking → Canning
  • Sun drying → Mechanized and sanitized dehydration
  • Cool storage → Sophisticated refrigerators and freezers
  • Blast freezing and individual quick freezing – less detrimental to nutritional and sensory quality

'Hurdle Technology': Combining techniques to create conditions that bacteria cannot overcome (e.g., combining drying with chemical preservatives and packaging, or mild heat treatment followed by packaging and refrigerated storage).

Purpose of Food Processing

  • Preservation: Extend the shelf life of food
  • Safety: Remove health hazards associated with microbial pathogens (e.g., pasteurization of milk)
  • Quality: Ensure delivery of foods of the highest quality (taste, aroma, texture, colour, nutrient content)
  • Availability: Ensure consumer access to a wide variety of foods at any time
  • Sustainability: Ensure resources are used most efficiently
  • Convenience: Allow foods to be consumed after limited preparation
  • Health and Wellness: Enhance nutritional value through fortification and enrichment

sessions.title: Contribution of Science and Technology to Health
indicators: Identify the contribution of science and technology to health
List some gadgets used to address health issues
sessions.content:
Health

Health refers to a state of complete emotional and physical well-being. A healthful diet, exercise, screening for diseases, and coping strategies can all enhance a person's health.

Contributions of Science and Technology to Health

  • Improved treatment for numerous illnesses and serious health conditions
  • Medical research leading to new medications and methods of treatment
  • Advanced surgical instruments and techniques
  • CT scanners and other diagnostic equipment
  • Prosthetic limbs and other assistive devices
  • Wearable fitness technology: Smartwatches can track steps, monitor heart rhythms, ECG, blood pressure, temperature, and heart rate
  • Electronic health records: Storing, managing, and transmitting data becomes easy and quick
  • Virtual reality devices – help ease symptoms of depression and anxiety
  • Augmented reality – helps surgeons perform complex operations
  • Medical tourism: Patients can get in touch with specialists anywhere in the world for consultations and second opinions

Medical Gadgets and Devices

  • Surgical instruments
  • Oxygen delivery devices
  • Laboratory equipment
  • Fingertip Pulse Oximeter – measures oxygen saturation in the blood
  • Infrared Thermometer – measures temperature without contact
  • ECG machines – monitor heart activity
  • Blood pressure monitors
  • Self-adhesive biosensor patches – track temperature and heart rate

sessions.title: Contribution of Science and Technology to Transport
indicators: Explain the meaning of transport
Give some examples of the mode of transport
Describe how people move from one location to the other
sessions.content:
Transport

Transport is the movement of people, animals, and goods from one location to another. Transport is important since it enables trade between people, which in turn establishes civilizations.

Modes of transport:

  • Air: Aeroplanes, helicopters, spacecraft
  • Rail: Trains, trams
  • Road: Wagons, bicycles, motor vehicles (motorcycles, cars, trucks, buses)
  • Water: Ships, boats
  • Cable: Cable cars, ski lifts
  • Pipeline: Oil, gas, and water pipelines
  • Space: Spacecraft
  • Amphibious: Hovercraft, screw-propelled vehicles

Contributions of Science and Technology to Transport

  • Faster movement: Individuals can move rapidly from one place to another
  • On-Demand Ride Services: Uber, Bolt, Lyft – summon a vehicle with an app
  • Sensors: Communicate with GPS services to determine the best route
  • Accident Prevention: Sensors alert drivers to the position of other vehicles and prevent collisions
  • Safety: Sensors in the seat belt can track the driver's physiological indicators and determine whether the driver is fatigued or intoxicated
  • Drones and all infrastructures made with electronic parts make transport safer

Negative Impacts of Transport

  • Transport is a major use of energy and burns most of the world's petroleum
  • Creates air pollution – nitrous oxides and particulates
  • Significant contributor to global warming – emission of carbon dioxide
  • Traffic congestion and automobile-oriented urban sprawl – consume natural habitats and agricultural lands

Solution: Reducing transport emissions globally will have significant positive effects on Earth's air quality, acid rain, smog, and climate change.

sessions.title: Contribution of Science and Technology to Information
indicators: Explain information
Describe the contribution of science and technology to information
Mention some devices that help in passing on information
Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of modern modes of communication of information
sessions.content:
Information

Information is data that has been processed in such a way as to be meaningful to the person who receives it. It is anything that is communicated.

Examples of information: Timetables, shopping lists, report cards, printed documents, pay slips, receipts, reports.

Contributions of Science and Technology to Information

  • Internet and e-mail – changed the way people communicate
  • Satellite TV – people can have access to all programs at the same time
  • People can get information easily about their cultures, what they wear, eat, and how they behave

Modern Modes of Communication of Information (MMCI)

  • Smartphones: Text, talk, email, and video calls
  • Social media
  • Video conferencing
  • Skype

Advantages of MMCI

  • Reduces the importance of distance: Enhanced mail-order retailing, integrated transport companies
  • Saves time and money: Much cheaper and quicker to transfer information
  • Instant communication: Contact anyone around the world through emails and text messages instantly
  • Face-to-face communication: Skype, social media sites, video conferencing
  • New and exciting jobs: System analysts, computer programmers, web designers, hardware and software developers
  • Global village: The universe has become a global village due to information technology

Disadvantages of MMCI

  • Job unemployment: Through outsourcing, downsizing, and redundancies (e.g., factories replacing skilled personnel with robots)
  • Hijack by unscrupulous persons: Cybercrime and malicious programs that can cause severe damage
  • Fraud: Online banking fraud, mobile money fraudsters
  • Lack of personal contact: People communicate online without knowing whom they are really talking to
  • Cultural erosion: Some cultures have consumed others – teenagers in Western countries have influenced teenagers in other parts of the world

sessions.title: Industrialisation
indicators: Explain the meaning of industrialisation
Discuss the contribution of science and technology to industrialisation
sessions.content:
Industrialisation

Industrialisation is the process by which an economy is transformed from primarily agricultural to one based on the manufacturing of goods. It is characterized by economic growth, a more efficient division of labour, and the use of technological innovation to solve problems.

Key characteristics of industrialisation:

  • Economic growth
  • More efficient division of labour
  • Use of technological innovation
  • Mechanisation of production
  • Growth of cities
  • Modernisation

Contributions of Science and Technology to Industrialisation

  • Development of large and small-scale industries
  • Mechanisation of production – factories have brought down prices of commodities, improved quality, and maximized output
  • Growth of cities – people have started moving toward industrial areas in search of employment
  • Modernisation – adoption of modern ways of life and values (food habits, dress habits, speaking styles, tastes, preferences, ideas, values, and recreational activities)
  • Improved development of transport and communication – leading to national and international trade on a large scale

Negative Effects of Industrialisation

  • Unemployment: Machines provide employment opportunities for men but they also take away jobs through labour-saving devices – technological unemployment
  • Weapons development: Different weapons have been developed which have brought fear and nervousness to mankind
  • Greater technological advancement means more risk for mankind

sessions.title: Local Industries
indicators: Describe the process involved in the preparation of Kenkey
Explain the process involved in the preparation of bread
Outline the process involved in the preparation of alcohol
sessions.content:
Local Industries

There are several local industries within our communities. Most of these local industries still rely on simple equipment and skills in their operation. Most of their products are produced from raw materials cultivated within the community.

Typical local industries: Kenkey industries, Bread industries, Alcohol industries.

Kenkey Production

Ingredients: Corn (maize), water

Process:

  • Preparation of corn dough:
    • Soak the dry corn in water
    • After three days, drain and wash the corn
    • Grind it and add some water to make it into a dough
    • Keep it in a warm place to ferment for two to three days
  • Kenkey preparation:
    • Knead the fermented dough until thoroughly mixed
    • In a large pot, bring water to a boil. Slowly add one part of the fermented dough. Cook for about 10 minutes, stirring constantly. This half is called the 'aflata'.
    • Combine the 'aflata' with the remaining uncooked dough
    • Divide into serving-sized pieces
    • Wrap tightly in banana leaves, corn husks, or foil
    • Place the wrapped packets on a wire rack above water in a large pot
    • Steam for one to three hours
    • Serve at room temperature

Bread Production

Ingredients: Yeast, warm water, sugar, salt, unsalted butter, all-purpose flour

Process:

  • Dissolve yeast in warm water, let sit for 5 minutes
  • Add remaining water, sugar, salt, butter, and flour, stir to combine
  • Knead until a soft ball of dough forms (about 7-10 minutes)
  • Place dough in a greased bowl, cover, and let rise until doubled in size (45 minutes to 1 hour)
  • Turn dough out onto a floured surface and gently press to remove air pockets
  • Divide dough into two, roll into a tight cylinder
  • Place into greased loaf pans, cover, and let rise until doubled (30-45 minutes)
  • Bake at 400°F (200°C) for 30-35 minutes
  • Remove from oven, brush with melted butter
  • Cool completely before slicing

Alcohol (Akpeteshie) Production

Alcohol is a colourless, volatile, flammable liquid produced by the natural fermentation of sugars and is the intoxicating constituent of wine, beer, spirits, and other drinks. It is distilled from palm wine or sugarcane juice.

Process:

  • Palm wine is first tapped and fermented in a large barrel, sometimes with yeast
  • Fires are built under the barrels to bring the liquid to a boil
  • Vapour is passed through a copper pipe within cooling barrels, where it condenses and drips into sieved jars
  • The boiled juice then undergoes distillation
  • The resulting spirit is between 40% and 50% alcohol by volume

sessions.title: Meaning of Portfolio and Reflective Practice
indicators: Describe the meaning of the portfolio
Explain reflective practice
sessions.content:
Meaning of Portfolio

A portfolio is a purposeful collection of students' work that exhibits the student's efforts, progress, and achievements in one or more areas.

Key features of a portfolio:

  • Purposeful collection of students' work reflecting efforts and achievements
  • Evidence of self-reflection
  • Develops reflective thinking at various levels
  • Includes an 'envelope of the mind' for exploring feelings, values, beliefs, and dispositions

Two major categories of a portfolio:

  • Collection of evidence: Student-generated artefacts recording events and experiences
  • Reflection: Student teachers reflecting on what has been learned

Purpose of portfolios in teacher education:

  • Encouraging student teachers to document and describe their skills and competence
  • Provides much richer information than traditional assessment methods
  • Stimulates students to develop new understanding and appreciation of their experiences
  • Recognizes links between different aspects of experiences
  • Formulates insights to be tested in future actions

Reflective Practice

Reflective practice is learning through and from experience towards gaining new insights into self and practice.

Key points:

  • Reflection allows a pre-service teacher to articulate and investigate personal goals and evaluate progress
  • Encourages a positive attitude to learn throughout life
  • Reflection involves engagement in critical and analytical thinking
  • A reflective individual is likely inclined towards life-long learning
  • Reflective thinking is central to students' self-evaluation

Portfolio writing embodies reflective inquiry – each item in the portfolio is reflected upon.

Self-reflection focuses on:

  • Problem areas
  • What has already been learned
  • What still needs to be learned
  • Plans for how new learning might take place

Self-reflection fosters metacognition – a person's awareness of his or her thinking – and is the cornerstone of the reflective portfolio.

sessions.title: Writing Portfolio for Students' Reflective Journal (SRJ)
indicators: Explain the meaning of the reflective journal
Describe reasons to write a reflective journal
Discuss the use of a reflective journal
sessions.content:
Reflective Journal

A reflective journal is a way of thinking critically and analytically about your work in progress. It shows how different aspects of your work interconnect.

A reflective journal is a place to write down your daily reflection entries – something good or bad that happened that you can self-reflect and learn from.

Key points:

  • Can help you identify important learning events that happened in your life
  • Events include: relationship, career, and personal life
  • Provides a better understanding of your thought process
  • Reflective journal notes prepared by students reflect what they understand
  • This activity develops self-correcting and self-evaluating skills

Reasons to Write a Reflective Journal

  • To make sense of things that happened: Writing down details may give you a perspective you may not have otherwise considered
  • To speculate as to why something is the way it is: Your views can come from your own common sense, or from something you have heard at a lecture or read in a book
  • To align future actions with your reflected values and experiences: Continue to observe the subject to decide whether to stick to your original views or make changes
  • To get thoughts and ideas out of your head: Writing down thoughts can help relieve pressure or help resolve problems
  • To share your thoughts and ideas with others: Getting opinions from others can help you clarify your feelings for a deeper understanding of yourself

How to Write Reflectively – The Three W's

What (Description)

  • Recall an event and write it down descriptively
  • What happened? Who was involved?

So What? (Interpretation)

  • Reflect and interpret the event
  • What is the most important/interesting/relevant/useful aspect?
  • How can it be explained? How is it similar to/different from others?

What's Next? (Outcome)

  • Conclude what you can learn from the event
  • How can it be applied in the future?

Difference Between Portfolio and Reflective Journal

  • Portfolio: A collection of artefacts
  • Reflective Journal: A space where students critically reflect upon their own learning process

Prompt questions or guided reflections: The tutor/lecturer provides the area of the course that they would like the student to reflect upon.

sessions.title: Checklist for Professional Portfolio Assessment (Pre-Service)
indicators: Outline the key areas of a checklist for professional portfolio assessment
Mention some evidence of growth as an indicator of pre-service teaching competencies
State some reflective behaviour and strategies to manage the learning environment
sessions.content:
Checklist for Professional Portfolio Assessment

It is important to determine the criteria for assessing portfolios. Assessment criteria which have been used to determine the quality of the student's performance should be clear and easy to understand.

A. Professional Preparation

  • Candidate's Information: First name, last name, academic year, semester, programme
  • Statement of personal teaching philosophy: Discussion of your growth as a professional
  • Resume (Curriculum Vitae)
  • Child abuse and neglect recognition reporting certificate and first aid training
  • Letters of recommendation
  • Evaluations: Observation, Teacher Candidate Internship

B. Pre-service Teaching Competencies

Evidence of growth in knowledge, understanding, and disposition includes:

  • Learner-centred and/or differentiated instruction providing for special needs learners
  • Instructional and curricular adaptations for multicultural, ethnic, socio-economic, and linguistic diversity, and gender equity
  • Developmentally appropriate instruction
  • Standards-based instruction
  • Essential knowledge, skills, and processes in the content area
  • Ability to deliver instruction that uses inquiry, critical thinking, and problem-based learning
  • Engagement of learners in studies of the nature of the respective disciplines
  • Authentic learning experiences providing connections to human values and endeavours
  • Integration of technology and media resources for instruction, classroom organization, and pupils' learning
  • Various formal and informal methods of assessment and evaluation of learners to inform instruction and learning

C. Reflective Behaviour and Strategies to Manage the Learning Environment

  • Reflect on an individual learner's behaviour or a classroom behaviour encountered during student teaching that was challenging
  • Document how you handled the situation
  • What type of strategies did you use?
  • What was the outcome? (Successes and frustrations)
  • Did you believe the overall outcome of your strategies was successful? (Why or why not?)
  • What areas of growth did you experience from implementing this plan and working with this learner and/or group?
